facts: Venus' surface temperature is 870°F, hot enough to melt lead
Its atmosphere is 92 times thicker than Earth's, equivalent to 1 mile of water depth
The pressure on Venus is 92 times that of Earth, similar to being 3,000 feet underwater
idea.payoff: and we still don't know why it's so extreme
idea.title: Venus: Hottest Planet 🌡
idea.description: Imagine a place where lead melts like ice cream 🍦, and the atmosphere is a crushing force 🌊. Welcome to Venus, the hottest planet in our solar system 🌡. But what makes it so extreme, and will we ever uncover its secrets? 🤔

scenes.visual: A stylized, hot orange-red planet (Venus) rotating slowly against a dark space background, with a heat haze effect.
A close-up of Venus's barren, rocky surface. A block of lead appears and visually melts into a puddle on the ground.

scenes.text: Atmosphere: 92x thicker than Earth (1 mile water deep)
scenes.visual: A side-by-side comparison of Earth with a thin blue atmosphere and Venus with an extremely thick, hazy, yellowish atmosphere. A graphic shows a depth meter for '1 mile water'.
An animation showing a deep-sea submersible or diver experiencing immense pressure, then transitioning to show a similar crushing force applied to a generic object on Venus's surface.

scenes.text: And we still don't know WHY it's so extreme.
scenes.visual: A wide shot of Venus, still shrouded in its thick, mysterious clouds, with a large, shimmering question mark overlaying it, emphasizing the unknown.
